claim.hypothesis: One clear prediction is that—as psychological threats—the experience of realistic and symbolic threats should each independently predict increased distress…and lower psychological well-being.
claim.hypothesis_location: Section: Unique and Sometimes Opposite Consequences of Realistic Versus Symbolic Threats ; p. 5.
claim.statement: Both types of threat (at T1) were positively associated with negative affect experienced over the course of a subsequent week.
claim.statement_location: Section: Study 3; Subsection: Psychological well-being; p. 25.

data.access_details: All questionnaires reported are available in the Supplemental Materials
data.notes: Took place from May 27-28 2020. The original sample consisted of 311 respondents before excluding participants based on pre-registered exclusion restrictions.

method.description: The authors use a structural equation to estimate the effect of realistic threat on negative affect.
method.steps: (1) Construct the survey and distribute it on Mturk; (2) Use confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) to examine the fit of the various potential models (for both two factor and one factor models); (3) Use a structural equation to estimate the effect of realistic threat on negative affect.
method.control_variables: Anxiety; COVID-19 – Impact Intrusion; COVID-19 – Impact Avoidance; Life Satisfaction; Positive Affect; Adherence to CDC Guidelines; Support for Public Health Initiatives to Reduce Spread of COVID-19; Social Identity Affirming Behaviors in Isolation

results.summary: Symbolic and realistic threat were positively associated with negative affect.

metadata.original_paper_title: Measuring Two Distinct Psychological Threats of COVID-19 and their Unique Impacts on Well-being and Adherence to Public Health Behaviors
